<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 3: Proposing a way forward

Your manager turns to you and says:

> “Where should we start?”

**Part A**: Write **5 bullet points** describing your approach. You must include:

- **one** bullet on what context/institutional detail you’d want to understand,
- **one** bullet on what data you’d want to find or request,
- **one** bullet on what analysis or method you’d try first,
- **one** bullet on what could go wrong / what might mislead you,
- **one** bullet on how you’d communicate results to a decision-maker.


**Part B**: Which TWO of the 8 Business Scientist skills will matter most here, and why?

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective** 
Students should:

Outline a realistic “first week on the job” investigation plan that connects institutional detail → data → first analysis → risks → communication.

Practice thinking like a business scientist by anticipating what could mislead them before drawing conclusions.

Identify which two of the 8 Business Scientist skills are most critical for this problem and explain why

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

Institutional detail drives measurement: you can’t analyze “surge manipulation” until you know how surge is computed (zones, refresh rate, inputs).

Data discovery as feasibility: good questions fail if you can’t get the right logs at the right granularity.

First analysis = a simple comparison: start with a clear “pattern to look for” (supply drop → surge spike) rather than complex models.

Analytical theory (what can go wrong): confounds (events/weather), outages, normal shifts, location mismeasurement, selection.

Communication is part of analysis: decision-makers need a concise story + one or two key exhibits + clear uncertainty/limits.

Link to the 8 skills: show how this task naturally activates multiple skills, but some matter more at the start.

💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

“What’s one institutional detail you must know before you can even define the behavior?”

“What’s the minimum dataset you’d need to do a first-pass check?”

“What is the very first pattern/comparison you’d look for in the data?”

“Name two plausible alternative explanations that could fool you.”

“If you had 60 seconds with an ops manager, what would your one chart be?”

“Which two skills are ‘binding constraints’ here, and why?”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

Jumping straight to solutions (“ban drivers / change the algorithm”) without defining what’s happening or how to detect it.

Asking for data that is too vague (“all Uber data”) or missing crucial granularity (no timestamps/location).

Proposing complicated methods before a simple first-pass check.

Treating “surge increased” as proof of manipulation without considering confounds (events, rain, outages, shift changes).

Ignoring how results will be communicated (no simple metric, no clear exhibit, no uncertainty).

Picking two skills with generic justification (“everything matters”) instead of naming what’s most binding early (often Data Discovery + Analytical Theory, or Institutional Detail + Creativity & Judgment depending on the group’s framing).
:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

Example solution -- there's many ways to approach this


**Part A**

Institutional detail: Clarify how surge pricing works in practice in Sydney/Melbourne (e.g., how “surge zones” are defined, how often prices update, and what driver actions affect the algorithm).

Data to request: Pull time-stamped logs on driver online/offline status by location, surge multipliers by zone, trip requests, wait times/ETAs, cancellations, and basic context (weather, major events).

First analysis: Look for “coordination events” — unusually sharp drops in active drivers within a small area over a short time window — and check whether these are followed by spikes in surge pricing compared with similar times/areas.

What could go wrong: Demand spikes (events, rain), app outages, traffic disruptions, or normal shift changes could create similar patterns, so we could falsely label normal market changes as manipulation.

How to communicate: Summarize findings in a short manager memo with one simple chart (surge vs driver supply over time), a clear estimate of how often these patterns occur, and a “confidence/limits” section explaining what we can and cannot conclude yet.

**Part B**

Data Discovery: Without the right logs (especially precise online/offline timing and location-level surge history), we can’t distinguish coordination from normal fluctuations.

Analytical Theory: This situation is full of ways to get fooled (confounds like weather/events, measurement limits, false positives), so we need to be explicit about what could go wrong and how we’d check robustness before recommending action.

:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
